content.text: Evaluation timing is as important as evaluation design. Running evals at the wrong moment — too early, too late, or too infrequently — produces misleading signals and wastes engineering effort. Understanding when to trigger evaluations across the development lifecycle turns evals from a one-time checkpoint into a continuous quality signal.
The Development Lifecycle and Evaluation Points

Every prompt-based system passes through recognizable phases, each demanding a different type of evaluation. The goal is not to run all evals at all times but to match the right evaluation to the right moment.

attrs.alt: Evaluation timing across the development lifecycle

content.text: Evaluations should begin before the first line of prompt is written. At the design phase, you are defining what success looks like — which means you are also defining what an eval measures.
For each acceptance criterion, write 2–5 concrete input/expected-output pairs. These become the seed eval set.
Ask: what would a wrong answer look like? Off-topic? Hallucinated? Misformatted? Document these as negative examples.
Decide now whether success is binary (correct/incorrect), graded (0–5), or reference-free (LLM judge). Changing metrics mid-project invalidates historical comparison.

Writing eval cases before writing the prompt forces clarity about what the system must actually do. It prevents the common trap of optimizing a prompt until it passes tests you invented after the fact.
The build phase is the most eval-intensive. Every iteration of a prompt deserves an eval run. The loop is tight: write prompt → run evals → read failure cases → revise prompt → repeat.

# Typical build-phase loop
python run_evals.py --prompt-file prompts/v3.txt --eval-set evals/dev.jsonl
# Review failures
python show_failures.py --run-id latest
# Edit prompt
vim prompts/v3.txt
# Re-run
python run_evals.py --prompt-file prompts/v3.txt --eval-set evals/dev.jsonl
Never touch the held-out test set during iteration. Peeking at test cases (even manually) inflates confidence.
Log prompt version, eval set version, scores, and timestamp. You will want to compare run 3 against run 17.
If the latest prompt version scores lower than the previous on any category, stop and investigate before continuing.
Before shipping to production, run the held-out test set exactly once. This is the official pre-deploy gate.
No category drops > 5 percentage points vs. prior release
All known failure modes tested with ≥ 1 example
P95 latency and per-call cost within agreed limits
No harmful outputs on adversarial inputs
If any gate fails, the release is blocked. The fix-and-retest cycle continues on the dev set; the held-out set is reserved for the next gate attempt.

A common mistake is running the held-out set multiple times during iteration "to see how close we are." Once you know the test scores, those examples are contaminated — your intuitions are now shaped by them.
Production evals operate differently from development evals. You cannot label every user request in real time, so you sample.
Review a labeled sample of production traffic at least weekly. Even 30 labeled examples reveal distribution drift.
Thumbs-down rates, retry rates, and session abandonment are fast proxies before labelers finish.
Set automated alerts when the sampled error rate exceeds your production baseline by more than a defined margin.
Beyond cadence-based evals, certain events mandate an immediate eval run regardless of schedule.
Same prompt can behave differently across model versions
Even minor wording changes can shift edge-case behavior
New capabilities introduce new failure modes
Users surfacing a problem the eval set did not cover
Interactions between features create unexpected behavior
The two-week deadline is a fallback, not a target. Any of the explicit triggers above should fire an eval run immediately.
Warning Signs That Evals Are Overdue
Teams often defer evals under time pressure. These signals indicate the backlog has become dangerous.
Prompt changed without an eval run:
Even a single word change to a system prompt is a code change. It should trigger evals the same way a code deployment does.
User-reported issues are the most expensive form of eval — they mean failures already reached users. Each complaint is a missing eval case.
Score hasn't been checked in > 2 weeks:
Drift accumulates silently. What was true of the model two weeks ago may not be true today if any upstream dependency changed.
Eval infrastructure added after a system is already in production is always harder to retrofit. Evals belong in the initial architecture, not as a follow-up project.
Team disagrees on whether the system is working:
If different engineers have different intuitions about quality, evals are the arbiter — the absence of shared metrics is itself a warning sign.

Running the first evaluation after code reaches production means all quality decisions were made on intuition. By the time failures are discovered, rollback is expensive and users have already been affected.

The fix is to treat eval readiness as a deployment prerequisite the same way tests are. If the CI pipeline blocks deployment on failing unit tests, it should also block on failing evals below the threshold.
Starting prompt iteration without a baseline score means you cannot measure whether changes helped or hurt. You are navigating without a map.

# Wrong: start iterating, then check
# run_evals(prompt_v5)  # What was v1?

# Right: establish baseline first
baseline = run_evals(prompt_v1)
print(f"Baseline: {baseline['accuracy']:.3f}")
# Now iterate against this number
Every eval run is most valuable relative to a prior run. Without the first data point, you have no direction.

Running evals once before launch and then never again treats quality as a static property. It is not. Models update, user behavior shifts, edge cases accumulate in production, and connector APIs change. An eval suite that is not re-run regularly provides false confidence.
Schedule the eval suite as a recurring CI job (daily or weekly), not just a pre-deploy step.
Treat new production failures as new eval cases:
When a user reports a bug, add their input to the eval set before fixing the prompt. This prevents regression.
Formalizing when evals run removes the decision from individual judgment and embeds it in process.
Full suite + new cases from production
The calendar does not replace judgment — if a trigger fires, you run evals regardless of where you are in the cadence. The calendar ensures evals happen even when no explicit trigger fires.
Modern evaluation infrastructure hooks into the same pipelines as code tests.

# .github/workflows/evals.yml
name: Evaluation Suite

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'evals/**'
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'  # Monday 6 AM — weekly cadence

jobs:
  fast-evals:
    name: Dev set (fast gate)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python run_evals.py --set evals/dev.jsonl --threshold 0.80
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}

  release-gate:
    name: Held-out test set (release only)
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python run_evals.py --set evals/test.jsonl --threshold 0.85
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
Fast dev-set evals run on every push; expensive held-out evals run only on tagged releases. This keeps CI fast while maintaining the release gate.
Trigger evals only when prompts or eval files change, not on every commit. This reduces noise and cost.
Encoding the threshold in the CI config means changes to the quality bar go through code review, not informal conversation.

How often to evaluate is a function of how fast the system changes, how costly failures are, and the size of the eval suite. There is no universal cadence — it must be calibrated to the specific system.
Legal, medical, or financial advice systems should eval more frequently than informational systems because failure costs are higher.
Teams shipping prompt changes daily need eval infrastructure that runs fast — sub-minute on the dev set so iteration is not gated by wait time.
Systems that rely on third-party APIs or retrieval systems should eval after any upstream change, which may happen on a schedule outside the team's control.
If the full suite takes 30 minutes to run, a fast subset (50–100 representative cases) lets teams get signal on every push while reserving the full suite for releases.
A prompt that has not changed in six months, running against a model with no version updates, can safely run evals on a monthly cadence.
A trivia assistant failing on 5% of questions causes little harm. A contract review system failing on 5% of clauses is a serious risk.
Some evals require human labelers or multi-model judges. These cannot run daily by cost alone. Use automated evals for high-frequency checks and expensive evals for release gates only.
Most mature eval setups separate the suite into tiers so that fast checks run on every change and expensive checks run on significant milestones.
The smoke tier is so fast that it fits in a pre-commit hook — it catches catastrophic regressions (empty outputs, crashes, format failures) before the change even enters CI.
A single eval score in isolation carries limited information. Tracking runs over time reveals trend lines and inflection points that single-point scores cannot show.

import sqlite3, json, datetime

def record_eval_run(db_path, run_info):
    """Persist an eval run to a local SQLite log for trend analysis."""
    conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    conn.execute("""
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS eval_runs (
            id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            ts TEXT,
            prompt_version TEXT,
            eval_set TEXT,
            accuracy REAL,
            categories TEXT
        )
    """)
    conn.execute("""
        INSERT INTO eval_runs (ts, prompt_version, eval_set, accuracy, categories)
        VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    """, (
        datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        run_info["prompt_version"],
        run_info["eval_set"],
        run_info["accuracy"],
        json.dumps(run_info.get("categories", {})),
    ))
    conn.commit()
    conn.close()

def show_trend(db_path, eval_set, last_n=10):
    """Print accuracy trend for the last N runs."""
    conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    rows = conn.execute("""
        SELECT ts, prompt_version, accuracy
        FROM eval_runs
        WHERE eval_set = ?
        ORDER BY ts DESC LIMIT ?
    """, (eval_set, last_n)).fetchall()
    for ts, pv, acc in reversed(rows):
        bar = "█" * int(acc * 20)
        print(f"{ts[:10]} {pv:12s} {acc:.3f} {bar}")
    conn.close()
Tag prompts with a version identifier (v1, v2, or a git commit hash) so eval history links to the exact prompt that produced each score.
Overall accuracy hides category regressions. A prompt that improves on 8 categories while degrading 2 may look like a win overall.
Automatically flag if the latest run drops more than N% on any category vs. the prior run, even if overall accuracy is stable or improving.
Before treating an eval run as authoritative, verify the following conditions hold.
Results are only comparable to prior runs using the same eval set
Confirm the held-out set was not used during this iteration cycle
Results must be traceable to a specific prompt artifact
Fewer than 50 cases makes statistical comparison unreliable
Changing the metric retroactively to explain a result is a form of p-hacking
Single-point results without a prior run to compare against have limited meaning
An eval run that fails any of these checks is not necessarily useless — it may still surface failure cases — but it should not be used to make go/no-go decisions.

Evaluation timing follows the development lifecycle: write test cases during design, run evals on every iteration during build, use the held-out set as a one-shot release gate, sample production traffic weekly, and trigger immediate re-evaluation on any model update, prompt change, or production failure. The three most common anti-patterns — eval after shipping, no baseline, and one-shot eval — all share the same root cause: treating evaluation as optional rather than structural. Embedding eval timing in CI pipelines and a formal calendar removes the decision from individual judgment and makes quality measurement a reliable property of the engineering process.
